text: Wemby's defensive rating dropped from 108.3 to 104.1 after the All-Star break. The difference is almost entirely in his help defense positioning — he's contesting 3.2 more shots per game at the rim and opponents are shooting 11% worse on those contests. You can see it in how he's staying vertical instead of biting on pump fakes.
label: analysis

text: Looking at the last 10 champions, 8 of them had a top-5 net rating in the regular season. The two exceptions (2023 Nuggets, 2021 Bucks) both had top-3 net ratings post-All-Star break. The Thunder finished 7th in net rating this year and were 9th post-ASB. Historically, that profile doesn't win titles.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's offense was historically great in the regular season (122.1 ORtg, 2nd all-time). But in the playoffs, it dropped to 114.3. The difference? Teams stopped doubling SGA and dared the role players to beat them — and they couldn't. Dort shot 29% from three, JDub shot 31%.
label: analysis

text: Wemby's block rate in the playoffs (8.7%) was actually lower than his regular season rate (9.3%). Teams adjusted by pulling him out to the perimeter and attacking the rim when he rotated. The Spurs countered by having Sochan play free safety, which is why Sochan's defensive rating improved by 6 points in the playoffs.
label: analysis

text: The Celtics took 48 threes in Game 5. That's more than they took in any regular season game. The Spurs adjusted by running them off the line in the second half — Boston went 3/14 from three after halftime.
label: analysis

text: People keep saying the Thunder choked but look at the numbers: OKC's half-court offense was 98.3 ORtg in the half-court vs. the Spurs, down from 104.1 in the regular season. The Spurs packed the paint and forced OKC to beat them with perimeter shooting from non-SGA players. Dort, JDub, and Wallace shot a combined 31% from three on 14.3 attempts per game. That's the series right there.
label: analysis

text: The Spurs' defensive strategy in this series was a masterclass. They used Wemby as a roamer rather than a primary defender on Chet, which let him contest shots from everywhere while Sochan and Vassell handled the point of attack. Chet shot 38% when Wemby was on the floor and 62% when he sat. The Thunder had no counter because they don't have a secondary creator who can punish a roaming big.
label: analysis

text: What makes Wemby's playoff run historically significant isn't just the counting stats — it's the efficiency and the defensive impact. He's averaging 28/14/5 with 4 blocks on 62% TS. The only players to match that scoring + efficiency + block combination in a single playoffs are peak Hakeem (1994, 1995) and Shaq (2000). And Wemby is doing it as a second-year player.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's heliocentric offense was always going to have a ceiling. In the regular season, SGA had a 34.7% usage rate — highest on any team that made the conference finals since 2019 Harden. But unlike Harden's Rockets, OKC didn't have the shooting around him to punish help. Only 2 Thunder rotation players shot above 37% from three in the playoffs. You can't run a heliocentric offense without spacing.
label: analysis

text: One underrated factor in this series: the Spurs dominated the possession game. They had a 16.2% offensive rebound rate to OKC's 9.1%, and they forced 2.8 more turnovers per game. That's roughly 8 extra possessions per game. In a series where 4 of 7 games were decided by single digits, those extra possessions were the difference between winning and losing.
label: analysis

text: Castle's development as a secondary creator is the biggest reason the Spurs made the leap from play-in to Finals. Last year he averaged 3.2 assists and shot 28% from three. This year: 6.1 assists, 36% from three, and he's been the primary ballhandler in crunch time while Wemby works off-ball. That takes immense pressure off a 7'5" center who shouldn't have to create his own shot every possession.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's defensive scheme actually worked reasonably well against Wemby individually — they held him to 44% shooting when guarded by Chet. The problem was everything else: Wemby's offensive rebounding (4.1 per game), his passing out of doubles (5.7 assists), and the Spurs' role players shooting 41% from three on Wemby kick-outs. You can't just 'stop Wemby' anymore — he's too complete.
label: analysis

text: Looking at the salary cap situation: Chet and JDub are both extension-eligible this summer. If OKC maxes both, they'll have ~$140M tied up in SGA/Chet/JDub starting in 2027-28. That's before filling out the rest of the roster. The new CBA makes it extremely punitive to have three max contracts — you lose the MLE, you can't aggregate salaries in trades, your draft picks freeze. Presti has some very hard decisions coming.
label: analysis

text: Spurs really went from 22 wins to the Finals in two years. Masterclass rebuild.
label: context

text: The Thunder are the first team in NBA history to win 68+ games and not make the conference finals.
label: context

text: Wemby with 14 blocks in the series is the most by any player in a Conference Finals since the NBA started tracking blocks in 1973-74.
label: context

text: People forget the Spurs were 22-60 two years ago. This isn't just improvement — it's the fastest turnaround in NBA history without signing a superstar free agent.
label: context

text: OKC's half-court offense was 98.3 ORtg vs Spurs compared to 104.1 in the regular season. The drop-off is entirely in the half-court — their transition offense was actually better in the playoffs.
label: analysis

text: The key adjustment from Game 4 onward: the Spurs stopped hedging on SGA pick-and-rolls and started switching everything 1-4 while keeping Wemby in drop coverage. SGA went from 32 PPG on 58% TS in Games 1-3 to 24 PPG on 49% TS in Games 4-7. The switch took away his midrange pull-up and forced him to finish over Wemby at the rim, where he shot just 41%.
label: analysis

text: Three numbers that explain the series: 1) OKC's role players shot 31% from three on 14.3 attempts per game. 2) The Spurs grabbed 34% of available offensive rebounds to OKC's 18%. 3) Wemby contested 18.2 shots per game — the most by any player in any playoff series since tracking began. The Thunder's offense was designed around SGA creating advantages, but the Spurs had an answer for every advantage he created.
label: analysis

text: One thing that's getting lost: the Spurs' guards were incredible defensively. Vassell and Castle held SGA to 6/22 shooting when they were the primary defender. They're both 6'5"+ with 6'10"+ wingspans, and they fought over every screen. SGA couldn't get the switch onto a weaker defender because the Spurs had no weak defenders to target.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's biggest roster flaw got exposed: they don't have a single player outside of SGA who can reliably create their own shot in the half-court. JDub is a good player but he's a connector, not a creator. When the Spurs took away the SGA advantage, OKC's offense devolved into contested pull-ups from role players. That's a roster construction issue, not an effort issue.
label: analysis

text: Watching the film, the Spurs ran a lot of 'delay' action with Wemby at the top of the key — basically using him as a 7'5" point center. This pulled Chet away from the rim and opened up cutting lanes for Vassell and Castle. The Spurs scored 1.18 PPP on possessions where Wemby touched the ball at the top of the key. That's an elite half-court number.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's defensive strategy of having Chet guard Wemby 1-on-1 without double-teaming was bold but ultimately failed. Wemby shot 48% when guarded by Chet, which isn't great, but he generated 5.7 assists per game out of those matchups — mostly to cutters and corner shooters. The Spurs scored 1.12 PPP on possessions where Chet was the primary defender on Wemby. You can live with Wemby scoring efficiently if you take away everything else, but the Thunder couldn't take away everything else.
label: analysis

text: Comparing this series to last year's WCF (Mavs-Wolves): the Mavs won because Luka and Kyrie could both create advantages and the Wolves had only one elite rim protector (Gobert). The Spurs won because Wemby is a one-man defensive system AND a one-man offensive engine. There's no historical comparison for a player who can anchor a top-3 defense while also being the hub of a top-5 offense.
label: analysis

text: Popovich's adjustment in Game 5 was subtle but brilliant: he started having Wemby set ball screens at the 3-point line instead of at the nail. This forced Chet to defend in space 25 feet from the basket, which is not his strength. Wemby as a pop threat from 28 feet is unguardable — Chet had to respect the shot, which opened up the entire floor for Castle and Vassell drives.
label: analysis

text: The Spurs outscored the Thunder by 12.3 points per 100 possessions when Wemby was on the floor. When he sat, the Thunder outscored the Spurs by 4.1. That's a 16.4 point swing. For context, Jokic's on/off in last year's championship run was +11.2. Giannis in 2021 was +9.8. What Wemby is doing at age 22 is unprecedented.
label: analysis

text: Let me break down why the 'OKC choked' narrative is lazy: the Spurs were the #1 defense post-All-Star break (107.2 DRTG). They had the best player in the series. They had home court advantage after Game 4. And they won Game 7 by 9 points. That's not a choke — that's the better team winning a close series. The Thunder didn't blow a 3-1 lead. They lost a competitive series to a team that figured them out.
label: analysis

text: Wemby's three-point shooting is the real X-factor that nobody talks about enough. He shot 37% from three in the playoffs on 5.2 attempts per game. When your 7'5" center can space the floor like that, it fundamentally breaks defensive schemes. You can't put your center on him because he'll drag them to the perimeter. You can't put a wing on him because he'll shoot over them. The only answer is to switch everything and hope he misses — and he didn't miss enough.
label: analysis

text: One tactical detail I haven't seen discussed: the Spurs ran a lot of inverted pick-and-roll with Castle as the screener for Wemby as the ballhandler. This forced OKC to switch a guard onto Wemby or give him a head of steam going downhill. Wemby scored 1.32 PPP as the ballhandler in pick-and-roll during the series — that's an elite number for ANY player, let alone a center.
label: analysis

text: The Spurs shot 39% from three as a team in this series. In the regular season they shot 35%. The difference? Wemby's gravity. When he rolled to the rim, OKC's help defenders had to collapse so hard that it left shooters wide open. The Spurs' role players got more 'clean' looks (defender 6+ feet away) in this series than they did all regular season.
label: analysis

text: SGA's free throw rate dropped from .452 in the regular season to .287 in the playoffs. Part of that is playoff officiating, but the bigger factor is that the Spurs defended him without reaching. Vassell and Castle stayed vertical, kept their hands back, and forced SGA to finish over length. SGA shot 51% at the rim in the regular season but just 41% at the rim in this series.
label: analysis

text: OKC's transition offense was actually elite in this series (1.21 PPP, would have led the league). The problem is they only got 12.3 transition possessions per game compared to 18.7 in the regular season. The Spurs' offensive rebounding and low turnover rate meant OKC couldn't get stops and run. You can't be a transition-reliant offense if the other team doesn't let you get stops.
label: analysis

text: Watching the tape, the single biggest difference between regular season OKC and playoff OKC was the disappearance of their drive-and-kick game. In the regular season, SGA and JDub drove and kicked for 22.3 three-point attempts per game (3rd in the NBA). Against the Spurs, that number dropped to 14.1. The Spurs' length at the point of attack (Vassell 6'5" with 6'10" wingspan, Castle 6'6" with 6'9" wingspan) meant OKC's drivers couldn't turn the corner and force help rotations.
label: analysis

text: Jeremy Sochan's offensive development is the most underrated storyline of this Spurs run. Two years ago he was a complete non-shooter (25% from three, teams didn't guard him). This year he shot 35% from the corner three and 72% from the line. He's not a sniper, but he's good enough that you can't just ignore him. That tiny improvement unlocked the Spurs' best lineups because they could play Sochan at the 4 next to Wemby without killing their spacing.
label: analysis

text: One thing about the Thunder that will get overlooked: they had the youngest rotation in the playoffs (average age 24.1). Every key player except Dort is 25 or under. This isn't a now-or-never situation — they have 7 first-round picks coming and all their core guys under contract. The window isn't closed. But they do need to figure out the half-court offense against elite defenses.
label: analysis

text: Watching OKC struggle against the Spurs' length reminded me of the 2019 Bucks losing to the Raptors. Giannis was the best player in the league but Toronto built a wall of long, athletic defenders (Kawhi, Siakam, Gasol, Green) and dared the supporting cast to beat them. OKC faces the same problem: SGA is incredible, but when a defense can throw 6'5"+ defenders at him AND protect the rim with a generational shot blocker, the math changes.
label: analysis

text: Let's talk about the Spurs' offensive rebounding because it was the hidden MVP of this series. They grabbed 34.1% of available offensive boards — that would have led the league by a wide margin. Wemby alone had 4.1 offensive rebounds per game, and about half of those turned into immediate putbacks or kick-out threes. The Thunder's smallball lineups with JDub at the 4 simply couldn't compete on the glass.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's defensive rating in the regular season: 106.1 (1st). Against the Spurs: 118.7. That's a 12.6 point swing — the equivalent of going from the best defense in the league to the worst. Some of that is Wemby being unguardable, but a lot of it is the Spurs' role players hitting shots they missed all season. Regression to the mean works both ways.
label: analysis

text: Castle's defense on SGA doesn't get enough credit. In 47 possessions where Castle was the primary defender, SGA shot 5/16 (31%) with 4 turnovers. Castle is 6'6" with a 6'9" wingspan and he's strong enough to absorb SGA's shoulder bumps without giving ground. He's already one of the 5 best perimeter defenders in the league as a rookie.
label: analysis

text: The Spurs' half-court offense was generating 1.08 PPP in this series, which is elite. They did it by running everything through Wemby at the elbow — from there he could shoot over Chet, drive past him, or find cutters. It's the same setup the Nuggets use with Jokic, except Wemby is 5 inches taller and a better shooter. The elbow series with a 7'5" hub who can shoot, pass, and drive is basketball's ultimate cheat code.
label: analysis

text: Looking ahead to the Finals: the Celtics have the personnel to guard Wemby better than anyone. Porzingis is 7'3" and can contest shots without jumping. Horford is one of the smartest post defenders ever. Tatum and Brown are both strong enough to switch onto Wemby for short stretches. But nobody has ever seen a player like Wemby in the Finals. The Celtics' defensive scheme will be the most interesting tactical battle we've seen in years.
label: analysis

text: One stat that sums up the Thunder's offensive problems: they averaged 0.92 points per half-court possession in the half-court against the Spurs. That would have ranked 29th in the NBA during the regular season. Their transition offense was great, but you can't run if you can't get stops, and they couldn't get stops because Wemby was generating efficient offense every trip down.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's pick-and-roll defense was excellent all season (0.88 PPP allowed, 2nd in NBA). Against the Spurs it ballooned to 1.05 PPP. The difference? Wemby as the roll man. When he rolls to the rim, he's 7'5" with a 8'0" wingspan catching lobs — you can't defend that with normal pick-and-roll coverage. OKC tried icing, hedging, switching, and dropping. Nothing worked consistently.
label: analysis

text: Can we talk about how the Spurs' rotation players all leveled up at the same time? Vassell went from 17 PPG on 55% TS to 21 PPG on 60% TS. Castle went from 12/4/4 to 16/5/6. Sochan went from a defensive specialist to a legitimate two-way player. This is what happens when you have a superstar who makes everyone better — the whole roster looks different.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's clutch offense was a disaster in this series. In the last 5 minutes of games within 5 points, OKC scored at a rate of 88.3 points per 100 possessions. For reference, the worst clutch offense in the regular season was around 95. SGA shot 4/14 in clutch situations. The Spurs' length bothered him more when the game slowed down and the refs swallowed their whistles.
label: analysis

text: Here's what I think OKC needs to do this offseason: 1) Find a legitimate secondary creator who can run pick-and-roll when SGA sits or gets blitzed. 2) Add more shooting — they need at least 3 above-average shooters around SGA at all times. 3) Figure out the Chet-at-center question. He's a great defender but he gets bullied by bigger centers. Maybe they need a true 5 and move Chet to the 4.
label: analysis

text: The Spurs' defensive gameplan against SGA was a clinic in modern NBA defense: 1) Fight over every screen (no going under). 2) Stay vertical at the rim (no reaching). 3) Force him left (he shoots 42% going left vs. 51% going right). 4) Live with contested midrange shots (he shot 38% on contested midrange in the series vs. 47% in the regular season). 5) Don't help off corner shooters (make him beat you with difficult shots). This is the blueprint for defending SGA, and every team will copy it next season.
label: analysis

text: The Thunder's offense was 1st in the NBA in the regular season (122.1 ORtg). Against the Spurs it was 112.4. The 9.7 point drop is the 3rd largest drop from regular season to a playoff series by a top-3 offense in the last 20 years. The two larger drops: 2015 Hawks vs. Cavs (-12.1) and 2018 Raptors vs. Cavs (-14.3). Both of those teams fired their coach within 2 seasons.
label: analysis

text: One thing I think the Thunder did well that's getting lost in the negativity: their defense on non-Wemby Spurs was actually good. The Spurs' role players shot 41% from the field when Wemby was off the floor. The problem is Wemby played 39 minutes per game. You can't scheme around a player who never sits.
label: analysis

text: Julian Champagnie's story is genuinely incredible. Undrafted in 2022. Cut by the 76ers. Signed to a two-way by the Spurs. Played 15 games in the G League. Now he's starting in the Conference Finals and playing credible defense on all-NBA wings. He's the type of player who only exists because the Spurs' development program is the best in the league.
label: context

text: Important context for the 'OKC has 7 first-round picks' narrative: under the new CBA, you can't roster 7 first-round picks. The Thunder have 15 guaranteed contracts next season plus 3 draft picks. They literally don't have roster spots for all their picks. Expect them to consolidate picks into a star trade this summer.
label: context

text: Spurs' path to the Finals: beat the #8 Grizzlies in 5, beat the #4 Nuggets in 6, beat the #2 Thunder in 7. That's the 8 seed, the 4 seed with the 3-time MVP, and the 2 seed with 68 wins. No easy path. They earned this.
label: context

text: The Thunder are the 5th team in NBA history to win 65+ games and not make the Finals. The others: 2007 Mavs (67 wins, lost 1st round), 2016 Spurs (67 wins, lost 2nd round), 2018 Rockets (65 wins, lost WCF), 2006 Pistons (64 wins, lost ECF). Wait, that's only 4? Let me check... yes, the 2006 Pistons won 64, not 65. So OKC might be the 4th or 5th depending on whether you count the 2009 Cavs (66 wins, lost ECF).
label: context

text: For those wondering about the Spurs' cap situation: they have Wemby on a rookie deal for 2 more years, Vassell at $27M/year declining, and everyone else on reasonable contracts. They can add a max free agent in 2027 without shedding salary. The future is terrifyingly bright.
label: context

text: Quick correction to something I've seen repeated: the Thunder do NOT own 7 first-round picks. They own 7 first-round picks OVER THE NEXT 3 YEARS. They actually have 11 tradable first-round picks total when you include picks through 2031. The distinction matters because they can't use all of them — they'll need to consolidate.
label: context

text: Wemby's playoff stats by series: Round 1 vs. Grizzlies: 26/13/4 with 4.5 blocks. Round 2 vs. Nuggets: 31/15/5 with 3.8 blocks. WCF vs. Thunder: 28/14/5 with 4.3 blocks. The consistency is absurd. He never had a bad game.
label: context

text: Not sure if this has been mentioned but the Spurs are the 2nd youngest team to ever make the Finals. Average age of their playoff rotation: 24.3. The youngest was the 2012 Thunder at 23.9. The difference is the 2012 Thunder had 3 future MVPs (KD, Russ, Harden). The Spurs have Wemby and a bunch of guys who were in the G League 2 years ago.
label: context

text: The Thunder's point differential in the regular season was +11.4 — 4th best in NBA history. Their point differential in this series: -2.3. That's a 13.7 point swing, which is the largest drop from regular season to a playoff series by any team with a top-5 all-time point differential.
label: context

text: I've seen some people say the Thunder should trade Chet. That's an overreaction. He's 23, he's on a rookie deal, and he's a top-25 player. But the fit with him as the sole big man against elite centers is questionable. The Thunder need a physical 5 (like Hartenstein but better) who can bang with guys like Wemby, Jokic, and Embiid while Chet plays the roaming 4 on defense.
label: analysis

text: Let me push back on the 'OKC needs to fire Daigneault' takes. He just won Coach of the Year. He took a team that won 24 games two years ago to 68 wins and the WCF Game 7. The loss hurts but firing a COTY because he lost a close series to a team with the best player in the world is how you become the Kings.
label: hot_take
notes: Borderline hot_take/analysis. Has reasoning but is primarily a rebuttal to a strawman ('fire Daigneault' takes) with opinionated framing → hot_take.

text: Wemby is the best player in the world and it's not a debate anymore. Jokic had his run. Giannis had his. This is Wemby's league now.

text: I'm so tired of the 'SGA is a free throw merchant' discourse. He averaged 8.7 FTA in the regular season and 7.1 in the playoffs. That's a normal drop for any star in the playoffs. The real story is the Spurs' defense, not the officiating.
label: hot_take
